stories.headline: Measles case confirmed at Denver International Airport and Littleton church
stories.summary:
A traveler with a confirmed measles case passed through Denver International Airport and attended a Sunday service at St. Mary's Catholic Church in Littleton while infectious, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment announced Tuesday. The passenger arrived Feb. 21 on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami, landing at gate A83 at 9:15 a.m., then attended a service and reception at St. Mary's, 6853 S. Prince St., from 9:30 a.m. to 2 p.m. Sunday. The traveler departed DIA the same night on Frontier flight 1280 to Miami, exposing anyone at the airport between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday.

Public health officials said the passenger is fully vaccinated, which lowers but does not eliminate the risk of transmission. Passengers on both flights are being notified by their state or local health agency. Free measles vaccination clinics were established Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department in Englewood and at St. Mary's Catholic Church.

Measles is a highly contagious but vaccine-preventable disease. Symptoms include fever, cough, runny nose and red eyes, followed by a spreading rash. Officials note that vaccination within 72 hours of exposure may prevent illness or reduce severity. Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the most in a single year since at least 1996, with most cases among unvaccinated individuals.

stories.headline: Grass fire injures five, prompts evacuations and highway closures in Thornton
stories.summary:
A fast-moving vegetation fire ignited Feb. 25 at 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton, spreading rapidly to the east under Red Flag wind and dry conditions before being fully contained at about 10 acres by 2:07 p.m. that day, Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley said at a press conference. The fire burned along a greenbelt between a residential neighborhood and a commercial zone, prompting law enforcement to conduct door-to-door evacuations and forcing the closure of several roads, including a stretch of Interstate 25 where smoke severely limited visibility. A nearby high school was also evacuated as a precaution.

Five people were injured in connection with the fire — four firefighters and one civilian — though Kelley described all injuries as non-critical. No residential structures were lost, though some businesses along the fire's southern edge sustained damage. Crews remained on scene overnight to monitor for reignition.

The response drew resources from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, North Denver Metro, Northglenn, Federal Heights, South Adams and other jurisdictions. Kelley described the event as a significant challenge for the region's northern fire departments. The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Man arrested in Kansas on suspicion of murder in Denver Valentine's Day shooting
stories.summary:
A 20-year-old man suspected of fatally shooting a man and injuring a woman at a Denver party on Valentine's Day was arrested Friday in Kansas, Denver police said. Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera was booked into the Wyandotte County Detention Center on a Denver homicide warrant and faces charges of first-degree murder, four counts of attempted first-degree murder, four counts of first-degree assault and a violent crime sentence enhancer, according to Denver court records.

Denver police responded to the shooting in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue at approximately 5:15 a.m. Feb. 14, finding a woman shot in the ankle and a man who died at the scene. Investigators believe a fight at a nearby party escalated into gunfire involving at least two shooters. Yriarte-Valera is believed to have fired the shot that killed the male victim; a second suspect, believed to have shot the female victim, had not been publicly identified as of Tuesday.

Yriarte-Valera's first Colorado court date had not been scheduled as of Tuesday. Anyone with information about the shooting is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Two defendants plead guilty to second-degree murder in 2024 Denver Aquarium shooting
stories.summary:
Jason Trujillo, 19, and Antonio Vasquez, 21, have each pleaded guilty to second-degree murder in the February 2024 drive-by shooting that killed 19-year-old Dacien Salazar outside the Denver Aquarium, the Denver District Attorney's Office confirmed. Both defendants are scheduled for sentencing March 6. They had originally been charged with first-degree murder after their arrest in May 2024.

Salazar, who had traveled from Pueblo with friends for Valentine's Day, was struck by gunfire from a black sedan that pulled up near the aquarium shortly after 3 p.m. Feb. 14, 2024. Investigators linked the shooting to prior Facebook threats made against Salazar and connected it to a separate, non-injurious shooting in Pueblo. Surveillance footage, cell phone location records and an anonymous tip helped identify the two suspects.

Vasquez entered his guilty plea Dec. 19, 2025, and Trujillo pleaded guilty Jan. 16, 2026, according to the DA's office. The case drew significant public attention after a bystander reported hearing multiple rounds fired in broad daylight near a popular family attraction.

stories.headline: Denver, Colorado business leaders respond to Supreme Court ruling striking down Trump tariffs
stories.summary:
Hours after the U.S. Supreme Court issued a 6-3 ruling striking down tariffs imposed by the Trump administration under emergency powers law, Denver and Colorado business leaders welcomed the decision as providing needed economic clarity. The Denver Metro Chamber of Commerce, the Colorado Chamber of Commerce and other groups issued statements emphasizing the importance of predictability and stability in trade policy for Colorado businesses, which had been affected across sectors including wheat, bicycles and automotive sales.

Denver Metro Chamber President and CEO J.J. Ament said the ruling "provides important clarity and helps establish the defined lanes needed for policymakers to work together toward thoughtful, consistent trade policies." Colorado Chamber President Loren Furman said many members had experienced supply chain disruptions, increased overhead costs and operational uncertainty as a result of the tariffs.

Some business leaders cautioned that the ruling may not mark the end of tariff pressure. Matthew Groves, president and CEO of one Colorado business group, noted the administration retains other avenues to impose tariffs and described the ruling as "temporary relief." President Trump announced plans to sign an executive order imposing a 10% global tariff rate on top of existing duties shortly after the decision was issued.

stories.headline: Laura Clellan named permanent director of Colorado Parks and Wildlife, first woman to hold role since 2011 merger
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners voted unanimously Monday to confirm Laura Clellan as the agency's permanent director, making her the first woman to lead CPW since the former parks and wildlife divisions merged in 2011. Clellan, who has served as acting director since Dec. 1, was chosen from approximately 150 applicants, with commissioners citing her leadership skills, her prior work with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Lakewood and her management of the agency during a period of significant transition.

Clellan brings more than three decades of military service, having achieved the rank of major general in the Colorado Army National Guard and previously serving as adjutant general and executive director of the Colorado Department of Military and Veterans Affairs, including overseas deployments to Afghanistan. Stakeholders from hunting and conservation groups offered cautious optimism about her leadership, though some questioned her specific wildlife management expertise.

Clellan assumes the directorship at a challenging moment for CPW. The state's wolf reintroduction program has been halted by federal wildlife officials, debates over furbearer management are ongoing, and some hunting groups have expressed concern about what they describe as diminished access and influence under the current administration. Her confirmation was met with a unanimous commission vote, with one absent member indicating he would also have voted in favor.

stories.headline: Denver Downtown Development Authority hires Urban Land Institute to chart future of Denver Pavilions
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has engaged the Urban Land Institute to conduct an advisory panel review of the Denver Pavilions mall and adjacent parking lots, with a team of five or six outside experts scheduled to visit April 13-17 and issue a report approximately one month later. The DDDA is paying ULI $100,000 for the assessment. The authority purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall at 500 16th St. in December for $37 million and acquired two adjacent parking lots from Brookfield Properties for $23 million.

The mall, which is approximately 60 percent leased, generates $5.3 million annually in rent and $3.2 million from parking operations. Gart Properties, from which the DDDA acquired the mall after a loan default, continues to manage the property for $379,000. The authority has also issued two requests for proposals — one for retail and entertainment leasing specialists and one for architecture and urban planning firms — with submissions due March 13.

Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said the DDDA's goal is to return the asset to private ownership as quickly as possible and preserve flexibility for whatever redevelopment vision emerges. "My hope is that we transfer this asset to the private sector as soon as possible and let them execute their vision," Mosher said. The Pavilions has struggled with vacancy and deferred maintenance, and its future is seen as central to the broader revitalization of upper downtown Denver.

stories.headline: Loretto Heights historic administration building to become 50 rent-to-own affordable apartments in $70M project
stories.summary:
Developer Commún plans to convert the historic Administration Building at the former Loretto Heights campus in southwest Denver into 50 rent-to-own apartments as part of a broader $70 million campus redevelopment effort. The project aims to provide an affordable pathway to homeownership, a model described as rare in Denver's housing market. The building's first two floors would be activated for community programming, including a business incubator, while upper floors would house the residential units.

A historic chapel connected to the administration building is planned to remain operational as a wedding and event venue, preserving one of the campus's most recognized architectural features. The Loretto Heights campus, a former Catholic college, has been the subject of ongoing redevelopment discussions for several years as the city and developers have worked to balance historic preservation with new housing needs.

The project reflects broader efforts to address Denver's housing affordability challenges through adaptive reuse of historic structures. Denver's construction costs rank second-highest nationally according to a 2026 report by the Beck Group, driven in part by luxury projects and complex downtown developments, making creative financing and reuse strategies increasingly significant for affordable housing production.

stories.headline: Heat pump installations in Colorado more than doubled in 2025, driven by rebates and contractor expertise
stories.summary:
Heat pump installations across the service areas of five major Colorado utilities more than doubled in 2025 to 14,225 units, representing a twelvefold increase over three years, according to utility and industry data. Xcel Energy alone issued 10,640 heat pump rebates worth $57 million last year, with Denver, Boulder and Littleton among the most active markets. Industry representatives say the technology has moved from niche to mainstream as contractors have become more proficient with installations and consumers have learned to stack available rebates to reduce upfront costs.

Air-source heat pumps, which can operate in subzero temperatures and serve as air conditioners in summer, typically cost $15,000 to $30,000 depending on the system and home size. In 2025, heat pump sales nationally surpassed gas and oil furnace sales for the second consecutive year. The Energy Efficiency Business Coalition has set a goal of heat pumps capturing 50 to 80 percent of the total heating and cooling market in Colorado by 2030.

Xcel Energy Vice President Emmett Romine said the product has gone from "a niche technology" to "a very widely accepted technology" in just a few years. The Platte River Power Authority, which serves Fort Collins, Longmont and other northern Colorado communities, saw its heat pump rebates nearly quadruple in 2025 to 1,256. Industry representatives describe 2025 as an inflection point that sets the stage for accelerating adoption through the rest of the decade.

stories.headline: $132 million affordable housing development breaks ground in Denver's Globeville neighborhood
stories.summary:
Evergreen Real Estate Group and Globeville Redevelopment Partners have broken ground on a $132 million mixed-use affordable housing development in Denver's Globeville neighborhood, transforming a city-owned parcel on the site of a former car dealership. The project will create housing for families earning between 30 and 80 percent of the area median income, targeting one of Denver's most historically underserved communities.

Globeville has long been at the center of debates over environmental justice and displacement, situated between major highway corridors and industrial land uses. The redevelopment of a long-vacant city-owned site into income-restricted housing represents a significant public and private investment in the neighborhood's future. Specific unit counts and a project completion timeline were not detailed in available disclosures.

The development is among the larger affordable housing investments announced in the Denver metro in recent years, arriving as the city continues to grapple with housing affordability and displacement pressures. The use of a brownfield site for affordable multifamily housing aligns with city goals to activate underutilized land while expanding access to homes at below-market rents.

stories.headline: Broncos reunite with John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator ahead of Payton's fourth season
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have hired John Morton as offensive passing-game coordinator, reuniting him with head coach Sean Payton after Morton spent the 2025 season as offensive coordinator with the Detroit Lions, a source confirmed to the Denver Post. Morton previously served in the same passing-game coordinator role in Denver in 2023 and 2024 before departing for Detroit, where he was stripped of play-calling duties midseason and fired after the Lions finished 9-8.

Morton had already been working informally with the Broncos for months, returning to the practice facility as a consultant during Denver's playoff run at Payton's invitation. In his first week back, Morton helped install a goal-line play for offensive tackle Frank Crum that resulted in a touchdown in the Broncos' 33-30 overtime divisional win over the Buffalo Bills.

The hire completes a significant offseason reshaping of Denver's offensive staff following the firing of longtime offensive coordinator Joe Lombardi two days after the Broncos' AFC Championship Game loss. Payton elevated former quarterbacks coach Davis Webb to offensive coordinator with likely play-calling duties, promoted Logan Kilgore to quarterbacks coach and hired Ronald Curry as wide-receivers coach. Morton has now worked on Payton's staff across three different tenures dating to Payton's first year with the New Orleans Saints in 2006.

stories.headline: MSU Denver launches fellowship to retain and support Black male educators in Denver and Aurora schools
stories.summary:
Metropolitan State University of Denver has launched the Black Male Educator Fellowship, known as "The Den," a program designed to provide professional development, networking and community support for Black male teachers working in Denver Public Schools and Aurora Public Schools. The fellowship is a joint effort between MSU Denver's Call Me MiSTER program, the university's Office of Education Solutions, the two school districts and the Colorado Men of Color Collaborative.

Program director Dr. Rashad Anderson cited persistent isolation as a key reason Black male educators leave the classroom, noting that Black residents represent approximately 6 percent of Colorado's population and that many Black teachers are the only educator of color in their building. According to the National Association of State Boards of Education, 62 percent of Colorado school districts do not employ a single Black male teacher, and 40 percent have no teacher of color at all.

Anderson said the fellowship aims to build a sense of brotherhood and professional community among participants, reducing the burnout and pressure that often accompanies being the sole representative of one's background in a school. Program participant Kaheem Heath, an aspiring teacher, said he has never had a Black male teacher in his own schooling and views the program as a critical pipeline for future educators. Organizers say diverse teaching staff benefit all students, not only those from underrepresented backgrounds.
